summary: This PR adds logic to rewind a readable/seekable request body before following a POST redirect (307/308), introducing `_body_position` tracking in `PreparedRequest` and a new `rewind_body` helper. The implementation is broadly correct for the happy path, but has several fragilities around the coupling between body-header purging and the rewindability guard, a security concern about replaying sensitive bodies to cross-host redirect targets, and a number of style, typo, and test-coverage gaps.

findings.title: No test for `_body_position` when `body.tell()` raises `ValueError`
findings.detail: `prepare_body` catches `(IOError, OSError, ValueError)` in the `tell()` guard, but the existing `test_rewind_body_failed_tell` test only exercises `OSError`. A test where `tell()` raises `ValueError` is missing, leaving that exception branch uncovered.

findings.title: Rewindability guard is fragile: tightly coupled to header-purging and lacks explicit body-None check
findings.detail: The `rewindable` predicate (lines 170-173) determines whether to rewind by checking for `Content-Length` or `Transfer-Encoding` in `headers`. This works correctly today only because those headers are also purged (lines 147-149) whenever `body` is set to `None` for non-307/308 redirects. The two code paths are silently coupled: if anyone removes or reorders the header-purging logic, `rewind_body` would be called on a `None` body, causing it to enter the else-branch and raise a misleading `UnrewindableBodyError`. Additionally, `rewind_body` itself has no guard against `prepared_request.body is None`. A more robust approach is to add an explicit `if prepared_request.body is None: return` at the top of `rewind_body`, and to add `and prepared_request.body is not None` to the `rewindable` condition in `resolve_redirects`. Separately, for 307/308 redirects where the body is kept and headers are not purged, the logic is correct — but the tight dependency on header state to infer stream-ness is fragile and should be documented.

findings.title: Rewound body is replayed to cross-host redirect target without restriction
findings.detail: When a 307/308 redirect crosses a trust boundary (e.g. internal host to an attacker-controlled external host), `rewind_body` faithfully rewinds and resends the full original request body to the new destination. `rebuild_auth` already strips the `Authorization` header on cross-host redirects, but the body itself (which may contain credentials, PII, or file contents) is not suppressed. An attacker who controls a server-side redirect `Location` header can therefore exfiltrate the entire POST body — a new attack surface introduced by this PR. Consider refusing to replay a streaming body on cross-host redirects, or at minimum documenting this behaviour prominently and providing a caller opt-out mechanism.

findings.title: Rewind path not tested when Transfer-Encoding is set or when neither body header is present
findings.detail: Two coverage gaps: (1) There is no test for a 307 redirect with a chunked/streamed body where `Transfer-Encoding` is set instead of `Content-Length`, leaving the `Transfer-Encoding` branch of the `rewindable` guard untested end-to-end. (2) There is no test exercising the case where a stream body is supplied but neither `Content-Length` nor `Transfer-Encoding` is present (e.g. a generator-based body), which means the silent skip of the rewind path in that scenario is unverified.

findings.title: No test confirming rewind is suppressed (not attempted) on 301/302/303 with a seekable body
findings.detail: There is no test verifying that a seekable body is NOT rewound (because `body` is set to `None`) when following a 301, 302, or 303 redirect, nor any test for a 308 redirect with a seekable body. These redirect codes share the rewind-related code paths and their behaviour (rewind for 308, suppress for 301/302/303) is currently unverified.

findings.title: Missing blank line before `UnrewindableBodyError` class definition
findings.detail: Every other exception class in this file is preceded by a blank line. `UnrewindableBodyError` is missing the blank line separating it from `RetryError`, inconsistent with the established file style.

findings.title: `PreparedRequest.copy()` propagation of `_body_position` is not tested
findings.detail: `copy()` now propagates `_body_position`, but the `test_prepared_copy` parametrized test does not include `_body_position` in the attributes it verifies. If the copy logic for `_body_position` were broken, no existing test would catch it.

findings.title: `rewind_body` conflates two independent failure modes (no `seek` vs. failed `tell`)
findings.detail: The two conditions in `rewind_body` — `body_seek is not None` and `isinstance(_body_position, int)` — each represent a distinct failure mode (missing `seek` attribute vs. `tell()` having failed at prepare time), but they are combined into a single if/else branch. When `_body_position` is the sentinel `object()` (tell failed) and `body_seek` is present, the code still reaches the else and raises `UnrewindableBodyError`, which is correct. However, the logic is hard to reason about and maintain. Splitting the two checks into separate, clearly labelled conditions (with comments explaining each failure mode) would improve long-term maintainability.

findings.title: Non-callable `tell` attribute passes guard; opaque `object()` sentinel is hard to maintain
findings.detail: Two related issues: (1) `getattr(body, 'tell', None) is not None` passes for any object with a `tell` attribute, including non-callable ones. A subsequent `body.tell()` call would then raise `TypeError`. The guard should be `callable(getattr(body, 'tell', None))`. In practice, file-like objects always have callable `tell`, so the risk is low but latent. (2) The sentinel for a failed `tell()` is a bare `object()` instance. Code in `rewind_body` distinguishes it via `isinstance(..., int)`. A named module-level sentinel constant (e.g. `_BODY_POSITION_UNSET = object()`) would make the intent explicit, prevent future callers from accidentally using the raw object as a seek offset, and is easier to inspect via hooks or subclasses.

findings.title: `rewind_body` not tested when `_body_position` is `None` (non-stream body)
findings.detail: When `_body_position` is `None` (non-stream body), `rewindable` is `False` and `rewind_body` is never called by `resolve_redirects`. However, if `rewind_body` is called directly with such a prepared request, the `isinstance(..., int)` check is `False` and `UnrewindableBodyError` is raised. There is no test verifying this edge case or confirming that the guard in `resolve_redirects` prevents the call for non-stream bodies.

findings.title: Rewindability check reads request headers — confirm no response-header bleed-through
findings.detail: The `rewindable` predicate checks `'Content-Length' in headers` where `headers = prepared_request.headers` (request-side). In the current code, response headers are never merged into the prepared request headers, so this is safe. However, the indirect dependency is subtle. A code comment clarifying that `headers` here refers to the outgoing request headers (not the redirect response headers) would prevent future confusion.

findings.title: Typo in docstring: "it's" should be "its"
findings.detail: The docstring reads "Move file pointer back to it's recorded starting position" — the apostrophe is wrong. The possessive form is "its" (no apostrophe).

findings.title: Typo in error message: "occured" should be "occurred" (also affects test assertion)
findings.detail: The error message "An error occured when rewinding request body for redirect." contains a misspelling of "occurred". The same typo appears verbatim in the test assertion at `tests/test_requests.py:1482` (`'error occured when rewinding request body'`), which would need to be corrected simultaneously. Note: the test assertion still passes today because the substring `'error occured'` (lowercase) is found inside the full message, but it would not catch the wrong branch raising.

findings.title: Use `is None` instead of `== None`
findings.detail: `assert prep._body_position == None` should be `assert prep._body_position is None`, consistent with the `is not None` idiom used throughout the same file.

findings.title: Missing blank line(s) before `Session` class definition
findings.detail: PEP 8 and the surrounding file style require two blank lines between top-level definitions. There is only one blank line between the end of `rewind_body` and the `Session` class definition.

findings.title: Unused variable `s` in `test_prepare_body_position_non_stream`
findings.detail: `s = requests.Session()` is assigned but never used in `test_prepare_body_position_non_stream`. This dead assignment is inconsistent with the lean style of surrounding tests and should be removed.

findings.title: Long lines in new 307 redirect tests
findings.detail: Lines in `test_HTTP_307_ALLOW_REDIRECT_POST` and `test_HTTP_307_ALLOW_REDIRECT_POST_WITH_SEEKABLE` exceed the ~88-character soft limit visible in surrounding test code. The `params` argument should be split onto its own line to match surrounding style.

findings.title: Rewind check uses `headers` which has already had Content-Length/Transfer-Encoding stripped
findings.detail:
For non-307/308 redirects (lines 145-150), `prepared_request.headers.pop('Content-Length', None)` and `prepared_request.headers.pop('Transfer-Encoding', None)` are called before `headers = prepared_request.headers` is assigned (line 152). The `headers` local variable used in the `rewindable` check (line 172) points to the same dict after the purge, so for a 301/302/303 redirect the body headers are gone and `rewindable` will always be `False` — even though `body` was also set to `None` (line 150) in that branch, so rewinding is moot there. The more subtle correctness concern is the inverse: for a 307/308 redirect the body is NOT cleared, the headers ARE kept, so `rewindable` is `True` and the rewind happens. That part is correct.

However there is a real ordering bug for the case where `rebuild_auth` (line 167) adds an `Authorization` header and an auth scheme that also sets `Content-Length` for the re-signed body — the rewind check runs AFTER `rebuild_auth`, reading the already-mutated headers. This is a minor sequencing fragility, not a crash, but worth documenting.

More importantly: the `rewindable` guard relies on the presence of `Content-Length` or `Transfer-Encoding` in `headers` to infer that the body is a stream. But `headers` is a `CaseInsensitiveDict` that was just potentially purged (for non-307/308). So for non-307/308 redirects where `body` was set to `None`, `_body_position` is still the old int value from the original prepare. If for some reason the headers were NOT purged (e.g. a future code path), the guard would incorrectly try to rewind a None body, which would raise `AttributeError` inside `rewind_body` because `getattr(None, 'seek', None)` returns `None` and the else-branch raises `UnrewindableBodyError`. This is acceptable behaviour but the logic coupling between the two branches is fragile.

findings.title: `rewind_body` raises `UnrewindableBodyError` for a body that legitimately has `tell` but no `seek`
findings.detail: When `_body_position` is a valid `int` (tell succeeded) but the body has no `seek` attribute, `body_seek` is `None` and the else-branch unconditionally raises `UnrewindableBodyError`. This is the intended design per the PR description, but the condition `isinstance(prepared_request._body_position, int)` does double duty: it both gates whether `tell` succeeded AND whether the position is usable. If `tell` succeeded (returning an int) but `seek` is absent, the error message says 'Unable to rewind request body for redirect' — which is correct. However the condition used is `body_seek is not None AND isinstance(_body_position, int)`. If `_body_position` is the sentinel `object()` (tell failed) AND body has a `seek`, the code will still enter the else-branch and raise — which is correct. But if `_body_position` is the sentinel `object()` AND body has no `seek`, it also raises, again correct. The logic is sound but the two conditions conflate two independent failure modes (no seek vs bad tell), making the code hard to reason about and maintain.

findings.title: `tell` is checked with `getattr(body, 'tell', None) is not None` but this also matches callable `tell` attributes set to a non-None non-callable value
findings.detail: The guard `getattr(body, 'tell', None) is not None` will be True for any object that has a `tell` attribute, including non-callable ones. It should be `callable(getattr(body, 'tell', None))` to avoid an eventual `TypeError` when `body.tell()` is called. In practice file-like objects always have a callable `tell`, so the risk is very low, but it is a latent correctness issue.

findings.title: Body is rewound even when `prepared_request.body` was set to `None` for non-307/308 redirects
findings.detail: For redirect status codes that are NOT 307 or 308 (e.g. 301, 302, 303), `prepared_request.body` is set to `None` at line 150. After that, `_body_position` is still a non-None int from the original `prepare_body` call. The `rewindable` check at line 170-173 tests `_body_position is not None` AND the presence of Content-Length/Transfer-Encoding in `headers`. Because those headers are also purged at line 147-149 for non-307/308, the check will correctly evaluate to `False` in that branch. This means the rewind is correctly skipped. BUT: this tight coupling means that if anyone removes or reorders the header-purging logic, body rewind would be attempted on a `None` body. A more robust guard would also check `prepared_request.body is not None`.

findings.title: `rewind_body` does not guard against `prepared_request.body` being `None`
findings.detail: `getattr(prepared_request.body, 'seek', None)` will silently return `None` when `body` is `None`, causing the else-branch to raise `UnrewindableBodyError`. If `rewind_body` is ever called when `body` is `None` (which the current `rewindable` guard tries to prevent via the header check, but is fragile), the error message will be misleading. Adding an explicit `if prepared_request.body is None: return` guard at the top of `rewind_body` would prevent this.

findings.title: Test assertion string does not match actual exception message capitalisation
findings.detail: The test at line 1482 asserts `'error occured when rewinding request body' in str(e)` (lowercase 'e' in 'error'). The actual message raised in `sessions.py` line 289 is `'An error occured when rewinding request body for redirect.'` which starts with capital 'A'. The `in` check is case-sensitive, so `'error occured'` (lowercase) IS a substring of `'An error occured'` — the assertion actually passes. However, the test would fail to catch a case where the wrong branch raises (e.g. 'Unable to rewind…') because 'error occured' is not in that message. This is a weak assertion but not an outright failure.

findings.title: Body rewound and re-sent to redirect target without verifying trust boundary
findings.detail: When a 307/308 redirect crosses a trust boundary (e.g. from an internal host to an external/attacker-controlled host), `rewind_body` faithfully rewinds and resends the full original request body to the new destination. The existing `rebuild_auth` call strips the `Authorization` header on cross-host redirects, but it does not suppress or truncate the body. A malicious redirect target (or a server-side-request-forgery scenario where the attacker controls the redirect Location header) can therefore exfiltrate the entire POST body, including sensitive payloads (credentials, PII, file contents). This is a new attack surface introduced by this PR; before this change, the file-like body was simply consumed/hanging and never replayed. Consider refusing to replay a streaming body when the redirect crosses to a different host, or at minimum documenting the security implication and giving callers a way to opt out.

findings.title: Rewindability check reads attacker-influenced response headers
findings.detail: The `rewindable` predicate checks `'Content-Length' in headers` and `'Transfer-Encoding' in headers`, where `headers` refers to the *request* headers of the prepared request (line 152: `headers = prepared_request.headers`). Those request headers are set during `prepare_body` and should be trusted. However, it is worth confirming that no earlier redirect-processing step copies or overwrites `prepared_request.headers` from the response headers (which are attacker-controlled). In the current code the response headers are not merged into the prepared request headers, so this is low risk, but the indirect dependency is subtle and worth noting in code comments.

findings.title: Sentinel `object()` for failed `tell()` leaks an exploitable type distinction
findings.detail: When `body.tell()` raises, `_body_position` is set to a bare `object()` instance as a sentinel. `rewind_body` then distinguishes the 'failed tell' case via `isinstance(prepared_request._body_position, int)`. This is fine functionally, but an external caller who introspects `_body_position` (e.g. via a hook or subclass) could be surprised by receiving an opaque object instead of `None` or a well-typed sentinel. More importantly, if future code inadvertently passes `_body_position` as a seek offset without the `isinstance` guard, it will pass a garbage object to `seek()`, potentially triggering unexpected behaviour in custom file-like objects. A named sentinel constant (e.g. `_UNSET = object()` at module level) would make this explicit and harder to misuse.

findings.title: Missing blank line before new exception class
findings.detail: Every other exception class in this file is preceded by a blank line. `UnrewindableBodyError` is missing the blank line between it and `RetryError`. The existing style sample shows consistent single-blank-line separation between class definitions.

findings.title: Typo in docstring: "it's" should be "its"
findings.detail: The docstring reads "Move file pointer back to it's recorded starting position" — the apostrophe is wrong; it should be the possessive "its" (no apostrophe).

findings.title: Typo in error message: "occured" should be "occurred"
findings.detail: "An error occured when rewinding request body for redirect." has a misspelling. This same typo surfaces in the test assertion at test_requests.py:1482 (`'error occured when rewinding request body'`), which would need to be corrected at the same time.

findings.title: Missing blank line before `Session` class definition
findings.detail: The rest of the file (and PEP 8 / the surrounding style) uses two blank lines between top-level definitions. There is only one blank line between the end of `rewind_body` and the `Session` class definition.

findings.title: Use `is None` instead of `== None`
findings.detail: `assert prep._body_position == None` should be `assert prep._body_position is None`, consistent with the `is not None` idiom used elsewhere in the same test file (e.g. line 1498).

findings.title: Unused variable `s` in `test_prepare_body_position_non_stream` and `test_rewind_body`
findings.detail: `s = requests.Session()` is assigned but never used in `test_prepare_body_position_non_stream` (lines 1406-1408) and is also unused in `test_rewind_body` before `s.rewind_body(prep)` — wait, it is used there. Only `test_prepare_body_position_non_stream` has a truly dead `s` assignment. Dead assignments are inconsistent with the lean style of surrounding tests.

findings.title: Long line in `test_HTTP_307_ALLOW_REDIRECT_POST_WITH_SEEKABLE`
findings.detail: Line 1176 (`r = requests.post(httpbin('redirect-to'), data=io.BytesIO(byte_str), params={'url': 'post', 'status_code': 307})`) is significantly longer than the ~88-character soft limit visible in the surrounding test code. Consider splitting `params` onto its own line.

findings.title: Long line in `test_HTTP_307_ALLOW_REDIRECT_POST`
findings.detail: Line 1169 (`r = requests.post(httpbin('redirect-to'), data='test', params={'url': 'post', 'status_code': 307})`) is noticeably longer than surrounding test lines. Consider wrapping it to match the surrounding style.

findings.title: Rewind logic never triggered when both Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding are absent
findings.detail: The `rewindable` check in `resolve_redirects` requires at least one of 'Content-Length' or 'Transfer-Encoding' to be present. There is no test that exercises the case where a stream body is supplied but neither header is present (e.g. a generator-based body without a known length), which means the rewind path is silently skipped in that situation. A test confirming the behaviour (no rewind attempted, no hang) for that branch is missing.

findings.title: No integration test for 307 redirect with a Transfer-Encoding chunked body that needs rewinding
findings.detail: The two new HTTP 307 integration tests cover (a) a plain string body and (b) a seekable BytesIO. There is no test for a chunked/streamed body where Transfer-Encoding is set instead of Content-Length, which is the other branch of the `rewindable` condition. This leaves the Transfer-Encoding branch of the guard expression untested end-to-end.

findings.title: No test for _body_position when body.tell() raises ValueError
findings.detail: In `prepare_body`, the except clause catches `(IOError, OSError, ValueError)`. The `test_rewind_body_failed_tell` test only exercises `OSError`. A test where `tell()` raises `ValueError` is missing, leaving that branch uncovered.

findings.title: PreparedRequest.copy() propagating _body_position is not directly tested
findings.detail: `copy()` now copies `_body_position`. The `test_prepared_copy` parametrized test does not include `_body_position` in the attributes it checks (it checks `method`, `url`, `headers`, `_cookies`, `body`, `hooks`). If the copy logic for `_body_position` were broken, no existing test would catch it.

findings.title: rewind_body not tested when _body_position is None (non-stream body path)
findings.detail: When `_body_position` is `None`, `rewindable` is `False` and `rewind_body` is never called. However, if `rewind_body` were called directly with a prepared request whose `_body_position` is `None` (e.g. non-stream body), the `isinstance(..., int)` check would be `False` and `UnrewindableBodyError` would be raised. There is no test verifying this edge-case or confirming the guard in `resolve_redirects` prevents the call for non-stream bodies.

findings.title: No test for rewind triggered during a 308 Permanent Redirect
findings.detail: The new rewind logic runs on any redirect (not just 307). Redirect codes 308, 301, 302, and 303 are not tested with a file-like seekable body to verify the rewind (or suppression of rewind after body stripping) behaviour. In particular, there is no test confirming that rewinding does NOT happen when the body is stripped on a 302 redirect.

findings.title: No test for `_body_position` when `body.tell()` raises `ValueError`
findings.detail: `prepare_body` catches `(IOError, OSError, ValueError)` in the `tell()` guard, but the existing `test_rewind_body_failed_tell` test only exercises `OSError`. A test where `tell()` raises `ValueError` is missing, leaving that exception branch uncovered.

(2) There is no test exercising the case where a stream body is supplied but neither `Content-Length` nor `Transfer-Encoding` is present (e.g. a generator-based body), which means the silent skip of the rewind path in that scenario is unverified.

findings.detail: There is no test verifying that a seekable body is NOT rewound (because `body` is set to `None`) when following a 301, 302, or 303 redirect, nor any test for a 308 redirect with a seekable body. These redirect codes share the rewind-related code paths and their behaviour (rewind for 308, suppress for 301/302/303) is currently unverified.

findings.detail: The error message "An error occured when rewinding request body for redirect." contains a misspelling of "occurred". The same typo appears verbatim in the test assertion at `tests/test_requests.py:1482` (`'error occured when rewinding request body'`), which would need to be corrected simultaneously. findings.detail: `assert prep._body_position == None` should be `assert prep._body_position is None`, consistent with the `is not None` idiom used throughout the same file.

findings.title: Missing blank line(s) before `Session` class definition
findings.detail: PEP 8 and the surrounding file style require two blank lines between top-level definitions. There is only one blank line between the end of `rewind_body` and the `Session` class definition.

findings.title: Unused variable `s` in `test_prepare_body_position_non_stream`
findings.detail: `s = requests.Session()` is assigned but never used in `test_prepare_body_position_non_stream`. This dead assignment is inconsistent with the lean style of surrounding tests and should be removed.

findings.title: Long lines in new 307 redirect tests
findings.detail: Lines in `test_HTTP_307_ALLOW_REDIRECT_POST` and `test_HTTP_307_ALLOW_REDIRECT_POST_WITH_SEEKABLE` exceed the ~88-character soft limit visible in surrounding test code. The `params` argument should be split onto its own line to match surrounding style.
